title: Planning a Web Project — Mapty Case Study

learningObjectives.text: Explain requirements and feature planning using a new example and justify the result.
Trace and explain user stories and flowcharts using a new example and justify the result.
Explain architecture planning using a new example and justify the result.
Explain project retrospective using a new example and justify the result.

whyThisMatters: Learners frequently use planning a web project — mapty case study without a clear model of why it behaves as it does; this lesson makes that model explicit. It belongs to the **Project Planning: Mapty** module.
mentalModel: Draw a decision map: **Requirements and feature planning** → **User stories and flowcharts** → **Architecture planning** → **Project retrospective**. Add branches where the learner must choose an alternative and label the evidence for the choice.

sections.title: Requirements and feature planning
sections.body: Start with user-visible outcomes and convert them into explicit behavior, inputs, edge cases, and acceptance conditions. Break large capabilities into end-to-end slices that can be implemented and verified independently.

sections.title: User stories and flowcharts
sections.body: User stories capture who needs a capability and why; flowcharts expose sequence and decisions. Together they reveal missing states before code is written.

sections.title: Architecture planning
sections.body: Architecture planning identifies data, responsibilities, boundaries, and communication paths. The goal is to reduce costly ambiguity about ownership and change, not predict every future file.

sections.title: Project retrospective
sections.body: A retrospective compares outcomes with goals, identifies successful decisions and accidental complexity, and records lessons that should influence the next project.

commonMistakes: Memorizing syntax without predicting the underlying value or execution order.
Mutating shared data when the task expects a new value or explicit ownership.
Treating browser APIs and language features as the same layer.
Choosing an abstraction from habit rather than required operations.
summary: Planning a Web Project — Mapty Case Study connects Requirements and feature planning, User stories and flowcharts, Architecture planning.
Use the topic to make behavior and decisions explicit rather than memorizing isolated syntax.
Verify understanding by explaining a new example and answering the topic quiz without looking back.

topicQuiz.failurePolicy: Show explanations, add missed objectives to local review, and allow immediate or later retry.

questions.prompt: A team is making a decision involving Planning a Web Project — Mapty Case Study. Which approach shows the strongest understanding of **Requirements and feature planning**?

choices.label: State the requirement and assumptions, apply the Requirements and feature planning model, and verify the observable consequence before adding complexity.
choices.feedback: Transferable understanding of Requirements and feature planning means using the model to make and verify a decision in a new context.

choices.label: Use the term Requirements and feature planning in documentation without changing any decision or behavior.

questions.prompt: Which statement best distinguishes **Requirements and feature planning** from **User stories and flowcharts** in this topic?

choices.label: They address different parts of the topic's model; a correct decision identifies which one explains the current requirement and how the two interact.
choices.feedback: The concepts are related but should be applied according to the requirement and the role each plays in Planning a Web Project — Mapty Case Study.

choices.label: They are interchangeable labels, so choosing between them has no effect on reasoning.

questions.prompt: A developer notices this recurring mistake: “Memorizing syntax without predicting the underlying value or execution order.” What is the best response?

choices.label: Return to the requirement, use the Planning a Web Project — Mapty Case Study model to identify the failed assumption, and verify the corrected behavior with a representative case.
choices.feedback: The useful response corrects both the misconception and the next development action for Planning a Web Project — Mapty Case Study.

choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Planning a Web Project — Mapty Case Study rule and the observable result.

questions.prompt: Which day-to-day workflow is most reliable when a task depends on Planning a Web Project — Mapty Case Study?

choices.label: Clarify the requirement, identify the relevant model or boundary, test the smallest representative case, then refactor after the behavior is understood.
choices.feedback: A reliable workflow makes assumptions and failures visible early, then improves the implementation after correctness is established.

choices.label: Start with the largest available abstraction and postpone defining expected behavior.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Planning a Web Project — Mapty Case Study rule and the observable result.

choices.label: Copy a solution from another project without checking whether its assumptions match.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Planning a Web Project — Mapty Case Study rule and the observable result.

choices.label: Optimize for line count first and inspect correctness only after release.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Planning a Web Project — Mapty Case Study rule and the observable result.

questions.explanation: A reliable workflow makes assumptions and failures visible early, then improves the implementation after correctness is established.
questions.hint: Prefer the sequence that produces evidence early and keeps decisions reversible.

questions.prompt: Which guideline shows the best engineering judgment about Planning a Web Project — Mapty Case Study?

choices.label: Use Planning a Web Project — Mapty Case Study to reduce uncertainty: document the requirement, choose a proportionate approach, and leave evidence that another developer can review.
choices.feedback: Use Planning a Web Project — Mapty Case Study to reduce uncertainty: document the requirement, choose a proportionate approach, and leave evidence that another developer can review.

choices.label: Use Planning a Web Project — Mapty Case Study in every file because more abstraction always improves software.

choices.label: Avoid Planning a Web Project — Mapty Case Study completely because every additional concept reduces maintainability.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Planning a Web Project — Mapty Case Study rule and the observable result.

choices.label: Choose only by the number of lines and ignore correctness, ownership, and future change.
choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Planning a Web Project — Mapty Case Study rule and the observable result.

questions.explanation: Use Planning a Web Project — Mapty Case Study to reduce uncertainty: document the requirement, choose a proportionate approach, and leave evidence that another developer can review.
questions.hint: Choose the option that balances correctness, clarity, cost, and the actual requirement.

assessmentProfile.label: Developer workflow and decision scenarios
assessmentProfile.rationale: The learner should make realistic development decisions using requirements, logs, file structures, configuration, or short code artifacts.
assessmentProfile.inLessonPolicy: Use concise concept checks; coding and hybrid topics include at least one code-reading check.
assessmentProfile.topicQuizPolicy: Questions Q04–Q08 form the topic quiz. The presentation mix follows this profile.
